moveAnnotations.annotation: This is the Alekhine Defense, one of the most provocative openings in chess. White plays 1.e4, claiming central space. The Alekhine invites White to overextend the center with pawn advances, then attacks the overextended pawns — a hypermodern strategy that requires precise timing.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Single e4 pawn in the center. White expects to maintain or expand this center.
plans: Build a strong center and develop pieces behind it
Respond to Black's provocations by advancing the center pawns

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays 1...Nf6, the Alekhine Defense — attacking e4 with a piece instead of a pawn. This daring move invites White to push e5, then d4, building a big center that Black will target as a structural weakness. The philosophy is: let White overextend, then punish.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: No pawn commitment from Black. The idea is to let White advance and then attack the resulting pawn.
plans: Provoke e5, then maneuver the knight to continue provoking advances
Undermine White's overextended center with ...d6, ...c5, or ...f6
alternatives: 1...e5 — classical and solid, doesn't provoke White's center

moveAnnotations.annotation: White pushes e5, accepting the challenge and gaining space while attacking the Nf6. This is the most natural response — the e5 pawn gains territory, opens the f1-a6 diagonal for the bishop, and forces the knight to move again.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The e5 pawn gains space but is advanced and potentially vulnerable.
plans: Continue advancing to build a massive center with d4, c4
Develop pieces quickly to support the advanced center
alternatives: 2.Nc3 — the Vienna-like approach, solid but lets Black equalize easily

moveAnnotations.annotation: The knight retreats to d5, a powerful central square. On d5, the knight controls b4, c3, e3, f4, and c7 — it's stable and well-placed. White will try to push c4 to kick it again, but for now the Nd5 is the strongest piece on the board.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: No pawn change yet. Black's strategy depends on attacking White's center with ...d6 soon.
plans: Play ...d6 to challenge the e5 pawn
If White plays c4, retreat to b6 and continue the provocation with ...d6
alternatives: 2...Ng8 — the Brooklyn Defense, retreating all the way back; too passive

moveAnnotations.annotation: White builds the two-pawn center with d4, supporting e5 and claiming dominant central space. Together, d4 and e5 control c5, d5, e5, and f5. This imposing center is exactly what the Alekhine invites — Black's plan is to prove it's overextended.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The d4-e5 pawn duo gives White a significant space advantage.
plans: Support the center with c4 to kick the knight and maintain space
Develop pieces behind the strong center

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays d6, striking at the e5 pawn — the head of White's pawn chain. This is the critical moment in the Alekhine: Black directly challenges the advanced center. After exd6 or dxe5, the pawn structure changes dramatically and Black begins the counterattack.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: After ...d6, the central tension is at its peak.
plans: Challenge and undermine White's center
Develop pieces to pressure e5 and d4
alternatives: 3...d6 could also be played on move 2, but Nd5 first is more flexible

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the knight to f3, entering the Modern Variation where White prioritizes piece development over further pawn advances. The Nf3 supports both d4 and e5, controls the g5 and e5 squares, and prepares kingside castling — a solid, positional approach.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White maintains the d4-e5 center. The position is less aggressive than the Four Pawns Attack but m.
plans: Castle kingside quickly and maintain a solid center
Play Be2 and develop harmoniously
alternatives: 4.c4 (Four Pawns Attack) — the most aggressive, chasing the knight with c4-c5...

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black pins the Nf3 with Bg4, a purposeful developing choice that creates immediate tactical pressure. The pin is real: if White plays Be2, Black can exchange Bxf3 and double White's f-pawns. The Bg4 also develops the bishop to an active square before Black plays ...e6.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: No structural change, but the pin on f3 limits White's ability to reinforce the center.
plans: Maintain the pin and develop the rest of the pieces
If White plays Be2, consider ...Bxe2 Qxe2 to damage White's structure
alternatives: 4...Bf5 — develops the bishop but doesn't create the pin pressure

moveAnnotations.annotation: White breaks the pin with Be2, unpinning the Nf3 and preparing to castle. The Be2 is the calm, positional response — it develops a piece, resolves the pin, and supports a solid kingside structure. After castling, White's position will be well-coordinated.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: No change. The center remains intact and White is ready to castle.
plans: Castle kingside and complete development
Play c4 later to expand on the queenside
alternatives: 5.h3 — forces the bishop to choose, but weakens the kingside slightly

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays e6, reinforcing d5 and preparing to develop the dark-squared bishop. The e6 pawn solidifies Black's central presence and creates the d5+e6 pawn formation that provides a stable base. The slight downside is that it locks the light-squared bishop behind the chain.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black creates a solid e6-d6 chain. The light-squared bishop will need to come back inside the chai.
plans: Develop ...Be7 and castle kingside
Prepare ...f6 or ...c5 to challenge the center
alternatives: 5...dxe5 — opens the center but gives White free development with dxe5

moveAnnotations.annotation: White castles kingside, completing development and connecting the rooks. With a strong center (d4+e5) and all pieces developed, White has a comfortable position with more space. The challenge for Black is to find a way to challenge White's center before it consolidates.
plans: Play c4 to expand and kick the d5 knight
Use the rook on f1 to support f4 if needed

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the bishop to e7, a modest but necessary square that prepares castling. The Be7 covers d6 and f6, supports the kingside, and gets the bishop off c8-f1 diagonal where it wasn't doing much. Castling is now the priority.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: No change. Black's setup with e6-d6 and Be7 is solid and prepares to castle.
plans: Castle kingside immediately
Prepare ...c5 or ...f6 to challenge the center
alternatives: 6...Bd6 — more ambitious, but the bishop can be hit by c4 and Nc3

moveAnnotations.annotation: White plays c4, attacking the Nd5 and gaining more central space. The c4 pawn forces the knight to retreat, expanding White's control of the center. After the knight moves, White will have a broad c4-d4-e5 pawn chain — maximum space advantage.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White now has pawns on c4, d4, and e5 — a massive pawn center.
plans: Drive the knight away and maintain the space advantage
Develop Nc3 and control the center completely

moveAnnotations.annotation: The knight retreats to b6, the standard square in this line. From b6, the knight eyes c4 and d5, staying relevant despite being pushed back. The knight can later reroute via c8-d6 or a4-c5 depending on the position's needs.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: No structural change. The knight on b6 puts pressure on c4 and d5.
plans: Castle and finish development
Play ...Bxf3 at the right moment to double White's pawns
alternatives: 7...Nb4 — aggressive but the knight gets hit by a3

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the knight to c3, completing minor piece development. The Nc3 controls d5 — the key central square that Black's knight just vacated — and supports the c4 and e4 pawns. White's development is now complete with a clear space advantage.
plans: Build on the space advantage with Be3, Rc1
Play for a d5 or f4 advance in the middlegame

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black castles, getting the king to safety. Both sides are now fully developed, and the Alekhine middlegame begins — White has more space but a potentially overextended center, while Black has a solid structure and will look for ...c5 or ...f6 to challenge White's pawns.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The position is stable: White has pawns on c4-d4-e5, Black has e6-d6.
plans: Play ...Bxf3 to double White's pawns and weaken the center
Prepare ...c5 or ...f6 to break down the pawn chain

moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White has a strong central pawn duo on e4 and d2.
plans: Complete piece development and prepare castling
Fight for central control with pawns and pieces

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black captures on c3, removing White's knight. This also delivers check, forcing the opponent to respond immediately. The bishop on c3 controls key diagonal squares and maintains active piece play.
White captures the bishop on c3. This pawn move supports a future d-pawn advance, a key central plan.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black moves the knight to c4. The knight reaches a powerful central outpost on c4, controlling multiple key squares.
The Scandinavian Variation is an important variation of the Alekhine's Defence. Black transposes into a structure with some Scandinavian flavor.
Black replies with Nf6. This is the defining move of the Scandinavian Variation — it shapes the pawn structure and determines the resulting plans.
e5 stakes a claim in the center. Central pawns control space and restrict the opponent's piece activity.
d4 stakes a claim in the center. Central pawns control space and restrict the opponent's piece activity.
dxe5 stakes a claim in the center. Central pawns control space and restrict the opponent's piece activity.
Nxe5 captures the pawn. This exchange changes the balance — White reconfigures the pawn structure or gains material.
Nxd7 captures the knight. This exchange changes the balance — White reconfigures the pawn structure or gains material.
Nb6 improves the knight's placement. The knight was less effective on d5 and moves to b6 where it serves the plan better.
g6 pushes on the kingside. This pawn advance gains space and can support a future attack toward the enemy king.
Bg7 — Black fianchettoes the bishop, placing it on the long diagonal where it controls the center from a distance. This is a key positional idea.
O-O. This is the typical position arising from the Scandinavian Variation. From here, understanding the strategic plans — piece placement, pawn breaks, and targets — is essential.
